name: Ruaumoko
description: Ruaumoko is the youngest son of Ranginui (the Sky Father) and Papatūānuku (the Earth Mother) in Māori mythology. He remained in his mother's womb when his siblings separated their parents to bring light into the world. He is the god of earthquakes, volcanoes, and geothermal activity. Still residing within Papatūānuku, Ruaumoko expresses his anger and sadness at the separation of his parents by shaking the earth, causing earthquakes. He is also responsible for volcanic eruptions and the creation of geothermal features like hot springs and geysers. Ruaumoko's constant movements and rumblings serve as a reminder of the primordial separation and the enduring connection between the sky and the earth. He is a powerful and respected figure, embodying the raw energy and destructive potential of the natural world, as well as the deep sorrow of familial separation. His actions shape the landscape and serve as a constant reminder of the profound forces at play beneath the surface of the world. He is a vital part of the Māori understanding of the natural world and its volatile nature, representing the ongoing dynamic between Ranginui and Papatūānuku.
subtitle: God of Earthquakes and Volcanoes

keyMyths.title: The Separation of Rangi and Papa
keyMyths.description: Ruaumoko remained within Papatūānuku when his brothers separated their parents. His movements within her womb cause earthquakes as he mourns the separation.

keyMyths.title: Ruaumoko's Anger
keyMyths.description: Ruaumoko's anger at the separation of his parents manifests as volcanic eruptions. These are seen as expressions of his deep sorrow and frustration.
keyMyths.source: Reed, Myths and Legends of Maoriland

keyMyths.title: Guardian of Geothermal Regions
keyMyths.description: Ruaumoko is also responsible for geothermal activity, creating hot springs and geysers. These are seen as manifestations of his inner power and connection to the earth.
keyMyths.source: Orbell, The Concise Encyclopedia of Māori Myth and Legend

sources.author: Sir George Grey

sources.text: A classic collection of Māori myths and legends.
